detail.reflection:
### What worked

The dual-critic on PR #1 (the bootstrap) caught the load-bearing
flaw in the original W1 plan: cycle-1 had W1-1 as "add --version
flag" but `linter/cli.py:36` already had `@click.version_option()`.
Both critics surfaced this independently — the problem-critic as a
no-op-patch concern, the assumption-critic as an unverified
assumption. The harness chain caught the flaw at the bootstrap
PR's review, BEFORE any code was written for the no-op unit.
That's the dual-critic earning its budget.

Empirically running `fabric-lint --version` revealed a real bug
(Click's package_name inference fails: `RuntimeError: 'linter' is
not installed`). The W1-1 unit pivoted from feature-add to
bug-fix WITHOUT changing the harness chain — the same contract
template, branch shape, dual-critic, retro discipline absorbed the
pivot transparently. The methodology was indifferent to the unit's
semantic shift.

The fix is genuinely tiny: one decorator argument. The harness
chain around it is the demo — contract → branch → impl → retro →
pre-PR → dual-critic → CI → merge. 30-line change, full ceremony,
zero menus surfaced to operator.

Cross-repo dfg-harness invocation worked: `uv run --project
/path/to/dfg-harness dfg validate --path .dfg --schemas
/path/to/dfg-harness/kit/SCHEMAS` validates tool-fabric's substrate
against external schemas. Fragile (machine-local paths) but
functional for a demo. The portability gap is filed as v0.3.1
liens (#2 dfg install, #3 schema portability).

### What broke

The bootstrap PR (#1) tried to ship a W1 plan whose first unit was
a no-op. Cycle-1 dual-critic caught it (BLOCKER from both critics);
cycle-2 reframed W1-1 as a real bug-fix using the empirical receipt
surfaced during the audit (`fabric-lint --version` raises
RuntimeError). The lesson: even a discovery-based unit selection
needs to be empirically validated before commit-1, not trusted from
README inference.

The cycle-1 plan.yaml stub had W2-W10 with empty `units: []` —
schema rejected it. Cycle-2 stripped W2-W10 from waves[] entirely
and demoted them to a comment block. Lesson: don't commit unfilled
plan slots; declare only what's actually decomposed.

Cross-repo invocation friction was real but not blocking. Every
`uv run --project ... dfg ...` command is verbose and machine-local.
Three v0.3.1 liens filed (#2/#3/#4) cover the structural fixes;
bridging via the verbose form is acceptable for a single demo PR.

### What you'd change

Pre-bootstrap empirical-validation step: before authoring the
W1 contract, run a 5-minute dogfood pass against the target's CLI /
test suite to surface any "obvious" assumption failures. The
cycle-1 audit caught it post-hoc; a pre-flight would catch it
pre-PR.

Invest in v0.3.1 lien #2 (dfg-harness installable) before W2 of
this repo. Without it, every contributor needs the same
machine-local workaround — the demo doesn't generalize.

Add an `empirical-receipt:` field to the contract template so the
reproduction case is canonical, not buried in a code-block. The
cycle-2 commit body was ad-hoc; codifying the format would lock in
the precedent the assumption-critic flagged.

Wave-gate ceremony for W1: the plan declares a checkpoint at
`.dfg/checkpoints/W1-gate.md` but the harness in tool-fabric has
no automation to author it post-merge. Either lay down a
`dfg wave close` invocation as a CONTRIBUTING note, or write the
checkpoint manually in a follow-up. Filed implicitly under v0.3.1
lien #2.

### Assumption to challenge

I assumed the dfg-harness chain would survive translation to
tool-fabric without code-side changes. Validated for THIS unit
(a tiny CLI bug-fix). NOT yet validated for: substrate-coherence
CI gate (no GH Actions in tool-fabric); auto-emit CriticVerdict
(would need #388-equivalent in tool-fabric); per-unit-retro CI gate
(also missing). The harness "shape" transferred; the harness
"enforcement teeth" did not — every gate ran inside this Claude
Code session, with the operator-as-auditor as the final
enforcement boundary.

This is honest about what was demonstrated: **harness as
methodology and substrate** transfers cleanly to a new repo.
**Harness as runtime CI/enforcement** requires per-repo wiring
(GH Actions + dfg installable) that this PR did not ship. That
scoping is the right thing to surface to a technical-stakeholder
audience reading the report.
